speaker: interviewer
text: It's the 10th of May, 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia. And the camera is ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we talk about the escape, Regina, could I just ask you, in the time that you had to yourselves in- at night, did you talk in general, or did you just try and sleep? Was there any kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes. We used, not to socialize, but but to talk, to- to keep-- we had friends together. Even to a certain time of night, before they switched the lights off, everybody had to be in the barracks, even with the men and the boys, we were outside our sleeping quarters. And-

speaker: interviewer
text: You could stay outside until a certain hour?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until a certain hour, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you spoke with the men and the boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the boys, yes, you know, because we couldn't speak like Dutch or German, but a lot of the Polish or from Czechoslovakia, they spoke really, really German. And we learned from Yiddish, you know, too, but we didn't want to speak Yiddish in the camp because we didn't want that the SS men will pick up some words. So when we were speaking about ourselves, it was in Polish. And the other nationalities were speaking in their own language. If they knew German, maybe there was German there too, from Germany. There was a girl, more than one, and men were there. One couple was a German couple. It was a beast too. And we used to be, like, together, you know. We used to help out each other. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was-- were there any organized lectures or people spoke about their life before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, but we knew all what was happened, and we couldn't speak to the Dutch people, because we wouldn't-- they wouldn't understand us. And the German, the one who could speak German, we used to-- to-- to-- oh, what's her name? She spoke so good German. And then Mrs. Schildkraut, she spoke German. And Mira-- oh, she spoke like a German. They used to socialize with the other, who could understand each other. It wasn't like in the big, big camp, maybe like in Auschwitz. We didn't steal from each other. We didn't hate each other. And whatever were working, we try just to be not-- maybe even friends. But we didn't-- help me out with- with the word. We were like-- like a big, big family. We didn't hate each other at all, because our only aim was to dream, to get out, and to tell-- we'll survive. But we were told later on, when they organized the escape, that even when one will survive of the nearly 500 people, the escape is going to be a success. So we never worried.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you spoke about escaping that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no. No, we were not--

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't even contemplate it then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not allowed to-- a word about-- we were told, when the time will come, you will be told. But in the meantime, nothing. Because you didn't know who will going to tell the Germans what's going on. So they had to be so careful amongst themselves, how it was going to be done and then why they did it. And it was a masterpiece.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there romances with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: With all of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, there were romances, too. But even that Dutch woman who survived and that Polish man, Engel, Chaim Engel and Zelma-- they got together in the camp. And he helped her to survive, because she would not have survived in Poland without a Polish language. And she wasn't well. She was sick for a little while. He almost carried her when the escape was on. Yes, and they survived. They are in the United States-- Massachusetts.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when Alexander Pechersky came with the Russian POWs, was that when the idea of escape started to--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was already. But they-- we didn't have any military help. And the boys needed somebody, you know, a little bit organization from the army. And they just came in time,

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they helped/

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that who organized the escape, his name was-- in a minute it will come to me. And they got together, and they-- they started to-- to organize it and work it out, where and what and-- Feldhendler, Leon Feldhendler. He was the one who started before the Russian came to think about-- if we are going to escape, they said, it has to be all or not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you knew him and listened to him speaking? No. We were not allowed to listen speaking. Everything was such in secret, that even when they were speaking amongst each other, one day they had, I think a-- they were playing chess or something, or similar to chess, and that's why [INAUDIBLE] they converted amongst themselves with Pechersky because they needed somebody who could speak Russian. And they managed somehow.

speaker: interviewee
text: But before that, that was in the third camp, because nobody who ever went near the third camp did not come back alive. And while I was sorting, an electrician came to fix up the lights in the barracks where we were sleeping. And he handed me a note. And that was from my brother, that he was in a third camp. And a certain day when he will come to get the lorry, because the food for the third camp was in such like a little wagon on rails, and that was pushed to the gates. And people from the other side opened the gate and took the food in. And then later on, they pushed the empty back. And a particular time, he will be collecting the-- that-- that wagon. If I could just carry a bundle to that window, already a packed-up bundle of clothes to that window, I'll be able to see him. It happened. I did see him. But I didn't recognize him. In those few months, from December to-- to June-- or was it maybe the beginning of May. And he grew taller, but it was my brother. But then in June, we heard during the night a lot of shooting. And next morning, it was Frenzel who came to tell us what it was, when the roll call was in the morning, that from the third camp, the boys were trying to escape. They had a tunnel or something like that, and they were all executed. My brother was amongst them. And later on, what I heard, but not everybody maybe knew about it, they didn't try to escape. Only a dog run over the mines, and a mine exploded. And they thought that they were trying to run away. If that's true, I don't know, because there were different versions of what happen, because we never even were allowed to get in contact with-- with-- with the third camp. Whatever went past the gate never came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew at that time that Teodor was in the third camp. Yes, I knew. I knew he was there. But you never knew what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was executed with all the others.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, at-- I mean, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And yes, yes. And that was the 27th of June. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Later I'll tell you an incident about the 27th of June when I was in Germany as a witness, but later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll remember or make a note.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. I remember that. You don't have to make a note.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So almost-- almost in the air, there was talk of escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was in the air.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was communication.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. And then we were told the night before, be prepared. And if we had a little bit of changing of clothes, to put it all under, so but it won't be suspected that we are a little bit bigger, because we were going, and the [?fourties?] of October was late autumn in-- in Europe, in Poland. So we had-- and I still had my coat. I looked after it. I didn't wear it. I used to have it under my head, that was almost like a pillow to sleep on it. And I- I had my coat that day. I took it with me, even if it was not so cold, but I had it on. And I had a scarf. And I had a little-- a little bag and a few cubes of sugar that German gave me-- I put into that bag. And by while I was running-- and yeah, something happened to that bag. But when we were going to march out the main gate, because going out any other way, the wires had to be cut, and they were-- there were current in it. And the mines had to be detonated because there were three fences. In between the second and the third fence, there were mines. So and to run to the forest, something had to be done before we could run there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the main gate wasn't affected with that. So we were going to march out when the roll call was on, instead to go to whatever we normally do to march out. And some of the boys had already rifles and guns, because in the meantime, they killed about 10 or 11 SS men. They were lured into the workplaces, and they never got out. And they weren't missed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not straight away. And when somebody ask about them, they just went somewhere and all that. But Frenzel was going in, too, and he changed his mind. And he came out. And he discovered-- and Bauer, another scharfuhrer, came from the third camp with a truck. And something had to be unloaded. I'm not so sure what it was there. And they couldn't find somebody because it was nearly roll call and finish of work. And then he went into an office, and he saw blood on-- on-- on a door or something. And that German was lying under the-- the desk, dead. And then went the alarms. And then the shooting started. And some of the boys and the men were impatient. They wanted to climb over the barbed wire, and they got shocked on there. And then the boys started to cut the wire through so we could run. And the shooting-- the most damage were done from the towers where the Ukraine guards were, because the Germans had only rifles or pistols. But the machine guns were up top in the towers, and there were four towers. So what the machine--

speaker: interviewer
text: So, in fact, roll call hadn't taken place yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was 10 minutes before when that tragedy happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And maybe we would have got out a lot, lot more of us. Maybe not all, but we would have-- not that many would have been killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started running. I wasn't far away from that opening they made in the fences. And on the way, there was somewhere a bush, because not only that, but from the camp to the forest, there was more than a kilometer clearing. And there was even some logs were lying there, and we had to jump over the logs and duck-- you know, not the shooting, just to the opposite direction of the shooting. And then my little bag with that little bit of sugar I had got caught on a branch, and there wasn't time to untangle it, so I just let it go. And when I stopped running in the forest, turned around-- there were a lot, a lot of us, hundreds, I should say-- there was only three of us. And then from Warsaw, Simon Rosemowicz and my late cousin, that was a second cousin-- she was in the knitting-- we were together from Siedliszcze

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zelda Metz from home, but Zelda Kelberman-- Metz Kelberman. She was married. She was in the United States. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we just stopped in the forest. We were looking for something to eat from the fields or something. We couldn't find. And then we saw there were some green tomatoes on-- on -- on something. We picked them up. There wasn't any water anyway. It was drizzling.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was dark?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was already night, middle of the night, or maybe not just the middle, but we kept on running. We didn't stop just in the beginning of the forest. And when we stopped, there was maybe about 20 kilometers from the-- the camp. And we picked those green things, whatever they were. Next morning, we found out that they were the fruit from the potato vines when they bloom. And they said it was poison, but it didn't poison us. We didn't-- the potatoes weren't there anymore because they were all dugged out. But those things were there. And then we managed to get to a farmhouse, and he took us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do, bang on the door?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they took us in, three only of us, and hid us in the barn where there was straw. And he ducked out a very, very big-- I don't know how to say it-- a place where three people could fit in, but far away from the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: He actually dug a hole?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where straw were-- straw. That was in the barn. And he gave us something to eat before we hid ourselves. And we had to take off some of our clothes to-- to dry them out. But we didn't give it to him. We just had them with us. Next day, the Germans came with the pitchforks, but it didn't reach us.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was able to dig such a big hole so quickly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three of us, yes. He maybe took it from the top.

speaker: interviewer
text: You helped him?

speaker: interviewee
text: We helped him. And we stayed there for three days. But after two days, Zelda said that she will go to Wlodawa. It wasn't far. And we knew somebody in the city council, a secretary. A son was our good friends, and that was a son of the people. Maybe they can help us to get some papers. We didn't have anything. But she didn't come back. And we didn't know what happened to her. So two days after, or a day after, we talked it over with Simon. We said, we leave. But only way that the farmer got in touch with other farmers, and they-- because we-- Simon paid with-- with the ring, with something else, because we didn't have any money. And they thought we had a lot of jewelry, because Simon managed, where he was working, to hide. And later on, he dug it up-- he was working-- and had it with him. And they-- they attacked us outside, a few kilometers from the farmhouse. He told us where to go to get out and all that. And he wanted to take my boots. And I said, no, I'm not giving. If you want, you have to kill me first, because I'm not going to run around in the cold without boots. And I had a watch, but the watch was hidden under mine cardigan thing, and they didn't detect the watch. And that stayed with me. And we walked and walked and walked, like around in a circle. We didn't know where we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just the two of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know where we were going, because it was all at nighttime. And later on, we decided we are not going to walk anymore at nighttime. We are going out at day. Whatever will happen will happen during--

speaker: interviewer
text: You and Simon?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the forest-- yes, in the forest. And I went to the main road to Wlodawa, and he stayed in-- in the forest. But it was those young pine trees, like we used to call them, schools-- you know, with the-- short, you can just crawl in there. You couldn't see him. And he hid there. And I walked out. And a young man was walking, and I ask him the road to Wlodawa. And he said he was going there. And he had a [?scythe?] on his shoulder. On the other side of the road, there were the Germans with machine guns and all that. And the German even said to that young man, so early and you're already on a rendezvous-- something like that. I understood him, and that-- you know. And I walked there with him and walked into Wlodawa. And I asked children where that, you know, [? Pan ?] Golebiowski lives. They showed me. When I got in there, the wife of that secretary was at home. She told me that Zelda was there. She left a note for me, and she was going home to their father, to the Golebiowski, to that man's father. And I said, I'll go there, too, and maybe I will find her. And we started to walk at daytime. I got almost home. I left Simon with a farmer's place, because I knew the people. And that farmer's--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did Simon join you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to the forest, and I got him. And then we walked together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Back to Wlodawa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And one day, Simon said, have I got a belt? I said, yes. What do you need a belt for? He said-- I've got a good belt. I want-- he want to hang himself. He didn't see a way out. And I said, if you would put the belt around my neck, I'll do the same, but I can't do it myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he walked on with me. And when we got closer to Siedliszcze, I left him with the-- on a farm. I knew the people. And she told me what happened to my grandparents-- grandmother and great-grandmother and all the other people who were collected. And they were all executed at a Jewish cemetery.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went home. The people were still living, you know. The place, it was almost evening. And they kept me there for one night. And I went opposite to a Mrs. Blusch. She had a brother with the same age what Simon was, and I was going to ask. Maybe she's got a paper-- not a paper, but a birth certificate, something so I can help out him. And then I got to know-- they arranged for me to see somebody I used to go to school with. And I knew the people, and she gave me her birth certificate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was Regina Wojciszyn. Yes, I've got it there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I can spell it for you. W-O-J-C-I-S-Z-Y-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had the same first name, Regina?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same first name, yes. And that helped me a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she gave you her birth certificate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Certificate. And when I went back to see Simon to give him what I have got, he wasn't there anymore. He left a note. He said he can't be a burden to me, because he will be like a millstone around my neck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he an older man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was about maybe 40-- 35, 40. And I didn't see him anymore, and I didn't hear about him anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never knew what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Zelda?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went the next day. I went very early to Mr. Golebiowski, and I ask him, was Zelda here? He said, yes. She was here, but she left. And would I like to stay a day? He gave me to-- to eat. But not-- I said I'm not going to stay inside the house, only that he can leave a door from the barn somewhere open, and I can crawl in somewhere, even when somebody will find me there, that they didn't know that I came. Because I couldn't put the people to danger. In Poland, there was not another country in Europe when they found a Jewish person with any other-- it could have been Ukraine or maybe-- maybe Polish, I don't know-- the whole family was executed. You couldn't do that to them. And then, next-- later on, I found out when I was in Germany that-- I was writing to them. Then he told me in a letter, the same night, Zelda was there. But he had us on a separate place. And he said in the letter, if you two would have teamed up, you would not have survived. You had to go your separate ways.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you agree with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We would not have survived together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Zelda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zelda went to-- to Lviv, to Lemberg. He helped her out with things. And I had the birth certificate. And I went a little bit further to other-- my parents' friends, to somebody with the name Alexander Jagieluk. They were very, very dear friends of ours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jagieluk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jagieluk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I write it down, then I'll spell it for you. But maybe it's a little bit too long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't worry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jagieluk. It's J-A-G-I-E-L-U-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not neighbors. They were in a different village. I have to walk quite a distance. The railway station was there. And I walked to them. And when they saw me, they nearly couldn't believe that I was alive. And they want to keep me there. And I said, I can't, because I don't want to endanger you. They had two young children, maybe eight or nine years old. And I said, if only you could help me in any other way. He said, yes. Before even he said that, I would like to get away to Germany, because in Germany, I won't be responsible for anybody, only for myself. And he said, yes, that's a good idea, because they have a niece already in Germany, in Frankfurt. And I knew her. She was a few years older. She was already married. I knew her from-- from home. And I got the address from them to Frankfurt. And they went to the-- he went to the station. He bought me a ticket to Lublin. And he said, there you are. He gave me 200 zloty and gave me something else to wear so I will have something to change. And-- But don't get off in Lublin. Get off one station before. That was Majdanek. That was the camp. But the camp was on a different-- not where the station was. But that day, my luck, the train didn't stop at Majdanek. It went right to Lublin, because in Lublin they had at every gate of the station-- it was a very big city and a very big station-- they had the Germans were standing there, and everybody had to show papers. In that particular time, we were not allowed to travel without special passes more than 30 kilometers. But from where I was coming to Lublin was 50 kilometers. And I couldn't-- I didn't have anything to show. But on that same train where I-- not a train, but in the- the same carriage, there was a lady from Chelm with two small children. And I offered my help. And she said, all she would like me to do is take the two girls down the carriage and stand on a corner where she will put all the-- her luggage there, the few parcels she had, and hold the children by the hand so they won't go away. And she went over to the guard to show her papers. And when everything was all right, she nod her head to me. And I just kicked-- there was a little gate-- kicked the little gate open, and I walked through with the two girls. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was the second miracle. There were other miracles before, but that's not so important.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she knew you had no papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: She didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But maybe the Germans saw that I am with her because I was holding her children and all that. I don't know. Anyhow, I went through. And I knew where the street-- where the employment office was. I make my way to-- to the [POLISH]. That was one of the posh streets in Lublin. And I saw, you know, [NON-ENGLISH]. So I walked in, because I was going then as a volunteer worker to Germany, because that birth certificate I have was born in Lutsk. And the Ukraines were murdering the Poles there. That was the eastern part of Poland. And I just thought of things, if I am from Lutsk and I was aware that they're working, I couldn't go back because I wasn't actually from the city. I was outside. And I've heard that something is happening there. And I just want to get away to-- to Germany. And I've got an address to Frankfurt. I knew the people. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Any they believed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't understand me. There was an elderly gentleman was sitting behind a very big desk. And he couldn't understand me. But the secretary came in, and she was so angry. How did I get in there? I said, a gentleman told-- called me in because the door was open. And what do I want? He wanted to know what I want. I said-- so I told her. And I gave him the address. He didn't ask for papers-- nothing. And he took out a sheet of paper. He wrote something, put the stamp on, and he said to go with those-- with that paper to Krochmalna Street. There is a-- a-- not a-- it was almost like a camp, but a transit place, the people who were going to Germany. And to wait there for a day or two, and I'll get all my papers, the ticket and some food, and I will be off to Germany. And that was when I left Lublin--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think it was the mention of Lutsk? Maybe the only word he understood was Lutsk and he knew--

speaker: interviewee
text: But he didn't ask. The only thing I said that I want to go as a volunteer worker to Germany, and I have an address to Frankfurt on Main, and the name of the people I'm going there, because they're already working there. And maybe there was on the address was the place where they were working, but I didn't know what it was. It was all, you know-- and I understood what that girl told him in German, because I understood German. I could even speak, but I couldn't let on. Because at that time of-- when I-- when they were teaching German at school, I was too young. And later on, they abandoned the German language, so I couldn't let on that I knew German. And my mother knew German, and, oh, my parents knew, and Russian and French and all that. But that's not so important.

speaker: interviewer
text: And most important thing is I had the paper. And I went to Krochmalna Street. There was another employment office. And I showed them who I-- not who I was-- I've got the paper. And they didn't know what to do with me. Would I like to sit down? Would I like a cup of tea? Or would I like to-- to clean my boots? Because I still had my boots. And I said, hold your horses. I'm only going to Germany. What's going on? Do you know from whom you've got that letter? I said, no, it was from the employment office. But it was all in Polish, because they were all Polish people there. No, they told me, he was something like from the whole district of Lublin-- the head. Did you ever know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. So they-- they didn't question me anymore-- nothing. And in that camp, I kept to myself, because there was a lot of Russian people. They were all escaping from-- from the Russian-- maybe more Ukraine than Russian, escaping. And they were all going to Germany. I don't know where.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Even if there were, I wasn't Jewish then. I finished, you know. And then the 2nd of November-- was it All Souls Day? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oh, and then they brought me the ticket and brought me some food, too, on the train. And I was off to-- to Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had to go from Lublin to Warsaw. In Warsaw I had to change because it was-- I had to stay a few hours at the station. From Warsaw, I got to [GERMAN]. Posen. In German, [GERMAN]. And I stayed there a half a night. And then I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where to go in Posen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I asked him, because a lot of people were going back after they leave-- Polish workers. When they had leave in Poland, they were going back to Germany. And they were almost on-- on the same-- not all of them, but some of them were going back. And I found out, you know, that the trains to go to Frankfurt-- like I had to go to Berlin first. And then I stayed there in Posen, something to eat. I still had a few few zloty with me. I-- I was sorry that I spent it all on cigarettes and something else then-- then because they would have changed the money then. The Polish zloty was half of a German mark. And on the border, they would have changed the money for me, but I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So whatever I could, I bought myself an apple. I bought myself a doughnut. And I had bread and something else they were giving to me. The food was so-- you know, I couldn't see myself not to taste normal food. And I was-- where I was before, with the friends of my parents, they were feeding me all right, because I got a little bit-- you know, I didn't look like I was starving.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from Posen, I went to [NON-ENGLISH], Bingen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the cigarettes for smoking or for using to sell?

speaker: interviewee
text: Smoking. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I went to Bingen, but in Polish it's [NON-ENGLISH]. And that was the border. And then I found out that I can change the money. And I didn't get much because I didn't leave myself that much to have a few more mark. And from there, I went to Berlin. In Berlin, we had to get out of the train because there was an air raid. We got into the bunkers-- bunkers you say in English. And then from-- when I came up, I got onto the wrong train. Instead to go to Halle, I went to Hanover. I couldn't change it. I had to-- to stop in Hanover. I got out. And I saw somebody, a young man with a P on the arm, because all the foreign workers had initials of the country. I think so, because that was for Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went over to him and asked him, you know, I have to go to Frankfurt. And I know I'm on the wrong track-- how to get via Halle to Frankfurt. And a German-- what is it called, like-- like the one who works there, but he was in that navy uniform, from the trains? And he talked to him in German, where I want to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I showed him, I had a red ticket that is [NON-ENGLISH]. And he said not to worry. At 2 o'clock in the morning, there will be a train from Hanover to [NON-ENGLISH], only for Germans. But if I go in there and don't speak to anybody and try to sit near a window where there are curtains, because it has to be camouflaged for the light, and pretend that you are asleep, and if they will come to ask you for the ticket, all they will do is ask you to get off the next station. That's all. And if not, you might get through.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, by 11 o'clock in the morning, you will be in Frankfurt, because it was a fast train, only four stops. And I managed. Nobody asked me-- asked me for the ticket. I managed to get through. Even a lady asked me, would I like something to drink? So I said danke schoen. Then I pretend I was sleeping, and I went through to Frankfurt. Did you manage to sit next to the window?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes, yes. I managed. And the curtain was covering my head. And I just went through. And then when I got-- that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape three [BACKROUND NOISE] of the interview with Regina Zielinski. It's the 10th of May, 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia.
